subsection.title: Definition
subsection.p: A quadratic form is a function built from degree-two terms.
In matrix form: \boxed{q(\mathbf x)=\mathbf x^T A\mathbf x.}
Usually A is taken to be symmetric.

subsection.title: Two-variable form
subsection.p:
For
\mathbf x= \begin{pmatrix} x\\ y \end{pmatrix},
and
A= \begin{pmatrix} a&b\\ b&c \end{pmatrix},
we get
\boxed{ \mathbf x^T A\mathbf x = ax^2+2bxy+cy^2. }

subsection.title: Symmetric part
subsection.p: So only the symmetric part of A matters for a quadratic form.

subsection.title: Positive definite
Negative definite
Indefinite
subsection.p: A quadratic form is indefinite if it takes both positive and negative values.

subsection.title: Semidefinite
subsection.p: Semidefinite forms can be zero on nonzero vectors.

subsection.title: 2\times2 test

table.title: Classification of a 2\times2 quadratic form by determinant.

subsection.title: Eigenvalue test
subsection.p: For a symmetric matrix A:

table.title: Classification of a quadratic form by the eigenvalues of A.

row.cell: all nonnegative, at least one zero
all nonpositive, at least one zero

subsection.title: Diagonalization of a quadratic form
subsection.p:
Let \mathbf y=Q^T\mathbf x. Then
\boxed{ \mathbf x^T A\mathbf x = \mathbf y^T D\mathbf y = \lambda_1 y_1^2+\cdots+\lambda_n y_n^2. }
The eigenvalues show the principal curvatures of the quadratic form.

subsection.title: Connection to Hessians
subsection.p: When mixed partials agree, f_{xy}=f_{yx}, so H_f is symmetric.

Near a critical point \mathbf p, the quadratic approximation is
\boxed{ f(\mathbf p+\mathbf h) \approx f(\mathbf p) + \frac12\mathbf h^T H_f(\mathbf p)\mathbf h. }

subsection.title: Second derivative test in two variables
subsection.p: Suppose \nabla f(a,b)=\mathbf 0.
Then:

table.title: Second derivative test for a critical point in two variables.

warning.p: A zero determinant in the Hessian test does not mean there is no maximum or minimum. It means the quadratic test does not decide.

The matrix for ax^2+bxy+cy^2 is
\begin{pmatrix} a & b/2\\ b/2 & c \end{pmatrix},
not
\begin{pmatrix} a & b\\ b & c \end{pmatrix}.
The form \mathbf x^T A\mathbf x uses a symmetric matrix when reading geometry.
